query: scientific evidence of SARS coronavirus binding to ACE2 receptor

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluating And Referring Patients For Outpatient Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus In The Emergency Department (Archived) -- Function -- Mechanism of Action of Monoclonal Antibodies Against SARS-CoV-2
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of COVID-19 is described by the entry of SARS-CoV-2, the causative virus, into the hosts' cells by binding the SARS-CoV-2 spike or S protein (S1) to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors expressed abundantly on the respiratory epithelium, such as type II alveolar epithelial cells. [9] This process is mediated by the receptor-binding domain (RBD) on the spike protein followed by priming of the spike protein (S2) by the host transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2) that facilitates cell entry and subsequent viral replication. [10] Monoclonal antibodies prevent the viral attachment by binding to a non-overlapping epitope on the surface spike protein RBD of SARS-CoV-2 with high affinity, thereby blocking the binding of the virus to the human ACE2 receptor.

retrieved_docs.title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies against COVID-19 disrupt host cell entry by binding the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (AE2) receptor binding domain (RBD) of the virus. SARS-CoV-2 engages the host cell ACE2 receptor via the spike (S) glycoprotein to cause infection. This makes spike S glycoprotein on the coronavirus surface a common target for antiviral therapy, most notable of which are monoclonal antibodies. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Bamlanivimab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: SARS-CoV-2 is a single-stranded enveloped RNA virus. The outer surface comprises the envelope, membrane, and many glycosylated S-proteins. The virus binds to the host cell's angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE-2) receptor via S-protein. The S-protein comprises 2 subunits, S1 and S2. With the help of the receptor-binding domain (RBD) of the S1 subunit, the virus binds to the ACE-2 receptor found in cells of the respiratory system, gastrointestinal system, and endothelium. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.content: The RBD is a fundamental peptide in the pathogenesis of infection as it represents a binding site for the human angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors. Inhibition of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) does not increase the risk of hospitalization for COVID-19 and severe disease. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Features, Evaluation, and Treatment of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: SARS-CoV-2 gains entry into the host cells by binding the SARS-CoV-2 spike or S protein (S1) to the ACE2 receptors in the respiratory epithelium. ACE2 receptors are also expressed by other organs such as the upper esophagus, enterocytes from the ileum, myocardial cells, proximal tubular cells of the kidney, and urothelial cells of the bladder. [47] The viral attachment process is followed by priming the spike protein S2 subunit by the host transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2) that facilitates cell entry and subsequent viral replication. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Features, Evaluation, and Treatment of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology -- Effect of SARS-CoV-2 on the Respiratory System
retrieved_docs.content: Increased vascular permeability and subsequent development of pulmonary edema in patients with severe COVID-19 are explained by multiple mechanisms. [51] [52] [53] These mechanisms include: Endotheliitis as a result of direct viral injury and perivascular inflammation leading to microvascular and microthrombi deposition Dysregulation of RAAS due to increased binding of the virus to the ACE2 receptors Activation of the kallikrein-bradykinin pathway, the activation of which enhances vascular permeability Enhanced epithelial cell contraction causes swelling of cells and disturbance of intercellular junctions The binding of SARS-CoV-2 to the Toll-Like Receptor (TLR) induces the release of pro-IL-1β, which mediates lung inflammation until fibrosis . [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention, Evaluation, and Management of Coronavirus (COVID-19) in Pregnancy and Puerperium -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The disease is caused by an encapsulated ribonucleic (RNA) virus – severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV2), which belongs to the family of viruses Coronaviridae. [8] This group of viruses also houses the SARS-CoV1 virus, the culprit for the previous ‘SARS’ pandemic in 2005 and the Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV). [9] The virus's origin remains a hotly debated topic, but 1 propounded theory involves a complex lineage involving wild bats, pangolins, and/or seafood. [10] [11] [12] The droplet-borne virus enters the respiratory tract and attaches to the ACE2 receptors in the human cells, allowing further entry into the cells. [13] Further replication within the cell and initiating an inflammation cascade results in the characteristic respiratory manifestations of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Many glycosylated S proteins layer the surface of SARS-CoV-2, helping the virus bind to the host cell's ACE2 (Angiotensin-converting enzyme-2 receptor) and facilitating viral entry. The S protein has 2 subunits: S1 and S2. The S1 subunit comprises NTD (N-terminal domain) and RBD (receptor-binding domain). [7] The RBD of the S1 subunit binds to the host cell's ACE2. The RBD is a primary target for producing neutralizing monoclonal antibodies. Etesevimab, bamlanivimab, casirivimab, and imdevimab are neutralizing monoclonal antibodies available against SARS-COV-2. Etesevimab and bamlanivimab bind to the overlapping epitopes in RBD (see Figures. Mechanism of Action of Etesevimab, Bamlanivimab, Mechanism of Action of Etesevimab). Casirivimab and imdevimab bind to non-overlapping epitopes of RBD. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Benefits and Risks of Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy for Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The novel coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2, is a positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus spread through respiratory droplets. [9] The virus gains entry into the cell by binding its spike protein to the angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) 2 receptors on host cells. [10] This receptor is found on the respiratory epithelium, upper esophagus, ileum, myocardium, proximal tubular cells in the kidney, and the urothelium of the bladder. After binding the ACE2 receptor, the virus can enter the cell, and viral replication can occur. [11]

retrieved_docs.content: The virus's transmission mode is mainly through infected individuals' respiratory droplets. [4] SARS-CoV-2 invades its host cells by binding its viral spike (S) protein - constituted of S1 and S2 spike glycoprotein subunits and located on its surface, to the human angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors expressed on the surface epithelium of various organs and transmembrane protease, serine 2 (TMPRSS2) receptors. [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Effect of SARS-CoV-2 on the Central Nervous System (CNS) There is emerging evidence of ACE2 receptors in human and mouse brains, indicating the potential brain infection by SARS-CoV-2. Zubair et al. outlined that neuroinvasion by SARS-CoV-2 can occur with various possible routes, such as transsynaptic transfer across infected neurons via the olfactory nerve, vascular endothelial cell infection, or migration of leukocytes across the blood-brain barrier. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: All five reported VOCs -Alpha(B.1.1.7); Beta(B.1.351); Gamma (P.1); Delta(B.1.617.2); and Omicron (B.1.1.529) have mutations in the RBD and the NTD, of which N501Y mutation located on the RBD is common to all variants except the Delta variant which results in increased affinity of the spike protein to ACE 2 receptors enhancing the viral attachment and its subsequent entry into the host cells. Along with NBD, RBD serves as the dominant neutralization target and facilitates antibody production in response to antisera or vaccines. [4] Two recent preprints reported that a single mutation of N501Y alone increases the affinity between RBD and ACE2 approximately ten times more than the ancestral strain (N501-RBD). Interestingly the binding affinity of the Beta (B.1.351) variant and Gamma (P.1) variant with mutations N417/K848/Y501-RBD and ACE2 was much lower than that of N501Y-RBD and ACE2. [5] [6] The mutations seen in Omicron are described below.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The SARS coronavirus (SARS-CoV) uses the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptor for entry into its host. [4] SARS-CoV recognizes the host ACE2 receptor through the virus's receptor-binding domain. Mutation in this domain may allow this and other coronaviruses to cause new and increased cross-species infections. [11] The virus concentrates in the lungs and small bowel, which are areas with a high density of its receptors. [4] Specifically, the alveolar epithelium shows the highest focus of infection. [4] The infection leads to serous pleural effusions, pulmonary edema, and consolidations with relative sparing of the upper respiratory tract. [4] The lung injury is thought to be due to an out-of-control immune response by the host leading to excess quantities of pro-inflammatory cytokines. [4] [12] Some patients will develop bacterial (i.e., Staphylococcus aureus, Strenotrophomonas maltophilia ), viral, or fungal (i.e., Aspergillus , Candida ) superinfections. [4] The histopathology features, which have been found during postmortem and may not be representative of all SARS cases, show epithelial cell proliferation, diffuse alveolar damage, and macrophage infiltration of the lung. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2214
retrieved_docs.content: NCIt COVID-19 Terminology encompasses a range of concepts relevant to the disease, including the antiretroviral drug ritonavir and the steroidal compound dehydroepiandrosteron. Dehydroepiandrosteron is classified as an anti‑inflammatory steroid, indicating that it belongs to the broader class of anti‑inflammatory steroids and may play a role in modulating inflammatory processes. By incorporating both ritonavir, which targets viral replication, and dehydroepiandrosteron, which can affect inflammation, the NCIt COVID-19 Terminology reflects the dual therapeutic focus on antiviral activity and control of the inflammatory response in the management of COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 317
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme metabolizes several clinically important drugs, including the chemotherapeutic agent paclitaxel, the anti‑arrhythmic drug amiodarone, and the proton‑pump inhibitor omeprazole. In addition to being a substrate for CYP3A4, amiodarone acts as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 isoform CYP2C9, potentially affecting the metabolism of other CYP2C9 substrates. Aspirin, the antiplatelet agent, is frequently co‑prescribed with the H₂‑receptor antagonist ranitidine and the cardiac glycoside digoxin; digoxin also shows a pattern of co‑occurrence with ranitidine. These three drugs—aspirin, digoxin, and ranitidine—are all included in the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, reflecting their relevance in the context of COVID‑19‑related therapeutic considerations. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 373
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] are defined by their antagonism of beta‑1 adrenergic receptors, and this pharmacologic class is also linked to the mechanism of action of amiodarone, which exhibits beta‑blocking activity among its multiple effects. Amiodarone is both a substrate and an inhibitor of several cytochrome P450 enzymes: the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes amiodarone, while amiodarone itself acts as an inhibitor of CYP2C9 and of the CYP3A subfamily (including CYP3A4). Both amiodarone and other drugs such as aspirin and omeprazole are listed within the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, indicating that they are recognized agents of interest in the context of COVID‑19 research and treatment considerations. Aspirin, which is also part of these COVID‑19 terminologies, is noted to co‑occur with digoxin, suggesting that the two medications are frequently mentioned together in clinical contexts or literature. Omeprazole, another agent included in the COVID‑19 terminologies, is metabolized by the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, highlighting a shared metabolic pathway with amiodarone. In addition to these pharmaceutical agents, the inorganic compound zinc sulfate (síran zinečnatý) is also included in both the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, reflecting its relevance as a potential therapeutic or supportive agent in COVID‑19‑related investigations. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how beta‑adrenergic antagonism, drug metabolism by CYP enzymes, drug‑drug co‑occurrence, and inclusion in disease‑specific terminologies interconnect to inform pharmacologic understanding and patient‑care strategies, especially in the setting of emerging infectious diseases such as COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2775
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is identified as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 CYP3A enzyme, a property that underlies its contraindication when used together with ritonavir. The interaction between amiodaron and ritonavir is classified as a contraindicated drug class combination, reflecting the risk of adverse effects due to CYP3A‑mediated drug‑drug interactions. Both amiodaron and ritonavir are listed as concepts within the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology, indicating that each agent is recognized in the context of COVID‑19–related pharmacotherapy. Understanding the inhibitory effect of amiodaron on CYP3A and its contraindication with ritonavir is essential for safe medication management, especially when these agents are considered for patients with COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 750
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is identified as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A, indicating that it can reduce the metabolic activity of these key drug‑metabolizing pathways. Because inhibition of CYP2C9 and CYP3A can lead to altered plasma concentrations of co‑administered medications, clinicians must consider potential drug‑drug interactions when prescribing amiodaron. Additionally, amiodaron is included in the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology, reflecting its relevance within the set of agents associated with COVID‑19 research or therapy. This dual characterization underscores the importance of recognizing amiodaron’s enzymatic inhibition profile and its contextual presence among COVID‑19–related pharmacologic concepts for informed patient care and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 467
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) metabolizes mikonazol, amiodarone, and omeprazole, while amiodarone and mikonazol both inhibit CYP2C9 and CYP3A, creating potential drug‑drug interactions for agents that rely on these pathways. Amiodarone is an anti‑arrhythmic used in cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation but is contraindicated with ritonavir because ritonavir strongly inhibits CYP3A4, raising amiodarone levels and toxicity risk. Acebutolol, a beta‑blocker, is also capable of treating cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, whereas digoxin is contraindicated in these life‑threatening arrhythmias. The NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology list several agents relevant to COVID‑19 management, including aspirin, clindamycin, kalcifediol, omeprazole, ritonavir, zinc sulfate, and dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA, classified in the ATC system as an other sex hormone/modulator of the genital system). Aspirin frequently co‑occurs with ranitidine and digoxin, and digoxin also co‑occurs with ranitidine, reflecting common prescribing patterns for cardiovascular conditions and gastrointestinal protection. Collectively, these metabolic relationships, inhibitory effects, contraindications, therapeutic uses, and co‑prescription patterns underscore the necessity of careful medication management when drugs share CYP450 pathways or are employed in complex clinical scenarios such as COVID‑19 infection and acute cardiac emergencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 335
retrieved_docs.content: Substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action represents a broad class of pharmacologic agents that block the activity of peptide hormone receptors. Within this overarching category, Substance with receptor antagonist mechanism of action (substance) is identified as a more specific subclass of agents that exert their therapeutic effect by antagonizing receptors in general. A particular example of this subclass is antagonisté CGRP receptorů, which comprises compounds that specifically inhibit the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor. Thus, CGRP receptor antagonists are situated under the umbrella of receptor‑antagonist substances, which in turn are encompassed by the larger group of peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists. This hierarchical organization clarifies how these drug classes relate to one another and aids clinicians in understanding the scope of receptor‑targeted therapies.
